hero.title:
Are you struggling with
substance use or dependence?
hero.sub: A free, confidential screening based on the Diagnostic and Statistical Manual of Mental Disorders, Fifth Edition. 11 questions. About 2 minutes.
picker.title: Choose a substance
picker.sub: Select what you want to evaluate.
info.dsm5.title: What is the DSM-5?
info.dsm5.p1: The DSM-5 (Diagnostic and Statistical Manual of Mental Disorders, Fifth Edition) is the authoritative guide published by the American Psychiatric Association for diagnosing mental disorders. First released in 2013, it was updated as the DSM-5-TR in 2022.
info.dsm5.p2: For substance-related conditions, the DSM-5 replaced the older distinction between "substance abuse" and "substance dependence" with a single diagnosis: Substance Use Disorder. Severity is measured on a continuum, making it more clinically useful than earlier editions.
info.criteria.title: The 11 DSM-5 criteria
info.criteria.sub: Each question in this quiz maps to one of these diagnostic criteria.
info.criteria.1: Using in larger amounts or longer than intended
info.criteria.2: Wanting to cut down but being unable to
info.criteria.3: Excessive time spent obtaining, using, or recovering
info.criteria.4: Cravings or strong urges to use
info.criteria.5: Failure to fulfill obligations at work, school, or home
info.criteria.7: Giving up or reducing important activities
info.criteria.8: Recurrent use in physically hazardous situations
info.criteria.9: Continued use despite physical or psychological harm
info.criteria.10: Tolerance: needing more for the same effect
info.criteria.11: Withdrawal symptoms when stopping or reducing use
info.severity.title: DSM-5 severity scale
info.faq.title: Frequently asked questions
info.faq.a1: The DSM-5 (Diagnostic and Statistical Manual of Mental Disorders, Fifth Edition) is the standard classification of mental disorders published by the American Psychiatric Association. It is used by clinicians, researchers, and insurance companies worldwide to diagnose and classify mental health conditions, including Substance Use Disorders.
info.faq.q2: What is a Substance Use Disorder according to the DSM-5?
info.faq.a2: The DSM-5 defines Substance Use Disorder (SUD) as a cluster of cognitive, behavioral, and physiological symptoms indicating that an individual continues using a substance despite significant substance-related problems. The DSM-5 identifies 11 criteria grouped into four categories: impaired control, social impairment, risky use, and pharmacological indicators (tolerance and withdrawal).
info.faq.q3: How does the DSM-5 measure addiction severity?
info.faq.a3: The DSM-5 uses a severity spectrum based on how many of the 11 criteria are met within a 12-month period. Meeting 2 to 3 criteria indicates a mild Substance Use Disorder, 4 to 5 criteria indicates moderate severity, and 6 or more criteria indicates a severe Substance Use Disorder.
info.faq.q4: Is this DSM-5 quiz a clinical diagnosis?
info.faq.a4: No. This quiz is a self-screening tool adapted from DSM-5 diagnostic criteria for educational purposes. A formal Substance Use Disorder diagnosis can only be made by a licensed clinician through a comprehensive clinical evaluation. If you are concerned about your substance use, please consult a qualified healthcare professional.
info.faq.q5: What are the 11 DSM-5 criteria for Substance Use Disorder?
info.faq.a5: The 11 DSM-5 criteria are: (1) using more than intended, (2) inability to cut down, (3) excessive time spent on substance-related activities, (4) cravings, (5) failure to fulfill major obligations, (6) continued use despite social problems, (7) giving up important activities, (8) use in physically hazardous situations, (9) continued use despite physical or psychological problems, (10) tolerance, and (11) withdrawal.
info.faq.q6: How long does the DSM-5 assessment take?
info.faq.a6: This DSM-5-based assessment consists of 11 questions and typically takes 2 to 3 minutes to complete. Each question corresponds to one of the 11 diagnostic criteria defined in the DSM-5 for Substance Use Disorder.
footer.disclaimer: This DSM-5-based screening is for educational and self-reflection purposes only. It does not constitute a clinical diagnosis. A formal diagnosis can only be made by a licensed clinician. If you are concerned about substance use, contact SAMHSA's National Helpline at 1-800-662-4357 (free, confidential, 24/7).
footer.cite1: American Psychiatric Association. (2013). Diagnostic and Statistical Manual of Mental Disorders (5th ed.). Arlington, VA: American Psychiatric Publishing. psychiatry.org/dsm
footer.cite2: American Psychiatric Association. (2022). Diagnostic and Statistical Manual of Mental Disorders, Fifth Edition, Text Revision (DSM-5-TR). Washington, DC: American Psychiatric Association Publishing.
quiz.hint: Answer based on your experience over the past 12 months. Responses are not stored.
results.title: Am I Addicted?
results.description: Your responses have been evaluated against the 11 diagnostic criteria defined in the DSM-5. This screening helps identify patterns that may indicate a substance use disorder.
results.disclaimer: This is a screening tool only and does not constitute a clinical diagnosis. For professional support contact SAMHSA at 1-800-662-4357.

question.1: Have you used [S] more often or in larger amounts than you originally intended?
question.2: Have you wanted to cut down or stop using [S] but found that you couldn't?
question.3: Have you spent a significant amount of time obtaining, using, or recovering from [S]?
question.4: Have you experienced cravings or a strong desire to use [S]?
question.5: Has your use of [S] resulted in a failure to fulfill major role obligations at work, school, or home?
question.6: Have you continued to use [S] despite it causing or worsening problems in your relationships?
question.7: Have you given up or reduced important social, occupational, or recreational activities because of [S]?
question.8: Have you used [S] in situations where it was physically hazardous to do so?
question.9: Have you continued to use [S] despite knowing it was causing or worsening a physical or psychological problem?
question.10: Have you experienced tolerance — needing more [S] than before to get the same effect?
question.11: Have you experienced withdrawal symptoms when you stopped or reduced your use of [S]?
result.none.msg: Your responses do not currently meet the DSM-5 threshold for a substance use disorder diagnosis. If you still have concerns about your use, speaking with a healthcare provider is always a worthwhile step.
result.mild.msg: Your responses suggest a mild substance use disorder based on DSM-5 criteria. This early stage is often the best time to seek guidance. Consider speaking with a healthcare professional or counselor about your patterns of use.
result.moderate.msg: Your responses indicate a moderate substance use disorder. This level of severity often benefits significantly from structured support. We encourage you to reach out to a licensed therapist, counselor, or addiction specialist.
result.severe.msg: Your responses reflect a severe substance use disorder based on DSM-5 criteria. Please consider reaching out to a healthcare provider, addiction treatment center, or crisis line as soon as possible. You do not have to navigate this alone.
